mappings.frame: Frame the interview around a product question (a job-to-be-done, a moment of friction, or a decision the team has to make). Tighten guide to 5–7 questions, run 6–8 sessions in 1 week.
Same observational craft — much shorter cycle. Two days in-field per site, three sites, and a one-page synthesis with photos and quotes within a week.
Replace opinion-polling with task-centered prompts. Bring stimuli (sketches, mocks) and have participants react, sort, and prioritize.
Industry doesn't have IRBs but does have privacy, legal, and DEI reviewers. Write a lightweight consent script and data-handling plan; bring it to legal.
Use the same coding discipline on a much faster cadence. Two analysts, one day, sticky notes (or Dovetail / Miro / Reduct).
Pair qual signals with product metrics. Define a 'success' metric before launch; instrument it; report effect with confidence and caveats.
Write tighter items, drop double-barrels, and target a behavior or attitude tied to a feature. Use MaxDiff or Kano if prioritization is the question.

mappings.uxr: Secondary research / desk research / lit review
mappings.frame: Same rigor, narrower scope. Build a one-pager that answers a product question with prior evidence — academic and industry — and notes what's missing.
Teaching translates to ResearchOps and stakeholder enablement: building self-serve templates, running clinics, and coaching PMs to ask better questions.
A research plan is a 1–2 page mini-proposal: question, why now, method, participants, timeline, decisions it will inform, risks. Same muscle, smaller deliverable.
Critiques in industry are real-time and verbal. Lead with the decision at stake, the strongest claim, and the weakest evidence. Skip the throat-clearing.
Highly transferable. Industry rewards practitioners who can pair n=8 interviews with n=800 surveys or product logs and write the through-line.
Policy thinking maps to strategic UXR: framing the problem space, mapping stakeholders, and informing roadmap-level decisions rather than feature-level ones.
Same craft. Shorter window (1–2 weeks), structured artifacts (photos, voice memos), and a debrief interview at the end.
Lean into this. Many UXR teams are starved for someone who can read a regression and pair it with a qual finding without losing either.

fallback.frame: Describe it in product terms: what question it answered, who you talked to, how long it took, and what changed because of it. That structure travels.
